claim: Ingots were reheated to 1200°C for 30 min, hot-rolled to 75% reduction at 1100°C and water quenched, then solution-treated at 1100°C for 2h under Ar and water quenched.
evidence_text: The cast ingots were reheated to 1200 °C for 30 min, hot-rolled to 75% thickness reduction at 1100 °C and water quenched. The hot-rolled materials were then solution-treated for 2 h at 1100 °C under Ar, and finally quenched in water.

claim: Short annealing was 450°C/1h for both steels; long annealing was 650°C/72h for 2Al and 600°C/24h for 8Al.
evidence_text: short annealing treatment (450 °C/1 h) and long annealing treatment (2Al: 650 °C/72 h; 8Al: 600 °C/24 h).

claim: The 2Al steel after short annealing exhibits yield stress of 420 MPa and uniform elongation of 75%; after long annealing, yield stress is 400 MPa and uniform elongation is 35%.
evidence_text: Interestingly, both annealing states exhibit identical flow behavior with a slight variation in yield stress (short annealing: YS = 420 MPa; long annealing: YS = 400 MPa). The main effect produced by the long annealing treatment is a significant reduction in the uniform elongation (short annealing: UE = 75%; long annealing: UE = 35%).

claim: The 8Al steel after short annealing shows a yield stress of 540 MPa, and after long annealing the yield stress increases to 990 MPa.
evidence_text: the long annealing treatment has a remarkable effect on both yield stress (short annealing: YS = 540 MPa; long annealing: YS = 990 MPa).

claim: The 2Al steel exhibits multiple-stage strain hardening with a pronounced decrease in slope at about 15% engineering strain.
evidence_text: The stress–strain curves of the annealed 2Al steels exhibit a pronounced variation in slope at about 15% strain.

claim: Tensile tests were performed at room temperature with an initial strain rate of 5×10⁻⁴ s⁻¹ on cylindrical specimens with 6 mm diameter and 40 mm gauge length.
evidence_text: Tensile tests were carried out at room temperature at an initial strain rate of 5×10^{-4} s^{-1} in a Zwick ZH 100 tensile machine. The tensile samples were cylindrical, with gauges of 6 mm diameter and 40 mm length.

claim: ECCI images were acquired under controlled diffraction conditions using an EBSD-based set-up to enhance dislocation and interface contrast.
evidence_text: We made use of a recently developed EBSD-based set-up to obtain ECCI images under controlled diffraction conditions that enabled enhanced dislocation and interface contrast.

claim: TEM thin foil samples were prepared by electropolishing in 30% nitric acid in methanol at -30°C and 10 V.
evidence_text: Thin foil samples were prepared by electropolishing using 30% nitric acid in methanol at -30°C and 10 V.

claim: The first hardening stage is determined by the size of the dislocation substructure (Taylor lattice structures, dislocation cells, cell blocks), with the flow stress contribution given by Δσ = G M K b / D, and the high carbon content (1.2 wt.%) promotes dislocation substructure formation by affecting dislocation cross-slip.
evidence_text: The first hardening stage is determined by the size of the dislocation substructure, namely, Taylor lattice structures, dislocation cells and cell blocks. The contribution of the dislocation substructure to the flow stress can be written as Δσ = G M K b / D ... The high carbon content (1.2 wt.%) plays a significant role in the formation of the dislocation substructure. This is ascribed to the role of solute carbon in dislocation cross-slip [4,19].

claim: The second hardening stage is caused by further refinement of the dislocation mean free path by deformation twinning (TWIP effect), and the twinning contribution to hardening is described by Δσ_twinning = K_H-P / (λ_twin)^(1/2), where average twin spacing λ_twin is the key microstructural parameter.
evidence_text: The second hardening stage is due to further refinement of the dislocation mean free path by deformation twinning (the twinning-induced plasticity effect). The contribution of twinning to hardening can be described by Δσ_ twinning = K_H-P / (λ_twin )^(1/2) ... Twin spacing is the key microstructural parameter of this hardening stage

claim: At 0.2 true strain, the long annealed 2Al steel contains dislocation cell blocks with an average size of 275±50 nm, which is the same as the 270±70 nm cell blocks in the short annealed 2Al steel strained to a true stress of 950 MPa, indicating that the strain hardening is controlled by identical dislocation substructure evolution.
evidence_text: At 0.2 true strain, the deformed structure of the grain interior of the long annealed 2Al steel consists mainly of dislocation cell blocks with an average size of 275±50 nm (Fig. 3(a)). The size of this dislocation substructure is the same as that found in the short annealed 2Al steel strained to the same stress level (cell block average size of 270±70 nm at true stress of 950 MPa [4]).

claim: After annealing at 650°C for 72 h, the grain interior of the long annealed 2Al steel remains essentially in the solid solution state, as it exhibits a similar yield strength to the short annealed alloy and its strain hardening is controlled by the same dislocation substructure.
evidence_text: As the short annealed alloy is in the solid solution state (c phase) [4] and both alloys contain a similar YS, we conclude that, in the annealing condition studied here, i.e. 650°C/72 h, the grain interior of the long annealed 2Al steel remains essentially in the solid solution state.

claim: ECCI reveals that the long annealed 2Al steel contains a uniform distribution of coarse incoherent M3C-type carbides along grain boundaries and annealing twin interfaces, which are approximately 1 μm wide and 3 μm long.
evidence_text: ECCI observations further reveal that the long annealed 2Al alloy contains a uniform distribution of coarse carbides along the grain boundaries and annealing twin interfaces, which are about 1 μm wide 3 μm long (Fig. 3(b)). According to recent thermodynamical calculations by Chin et al. [23] on the stability of carbides in the Fe–Mn–Al–C system, the intergranular carbides observed in the long annealed 2Al steel correspond to incoherent M3C-type carbides.

claim: The estimated twinning stress is 670–750 MPa (assuming γ=60 mJ/m², b=0.25 nm, Taylor factor 2.8–3.2), leading to twin bundles; impingement of twin tips on coarse M3C-type carbides creates local shear stress concentrations that promote intergranular fracture.
evidence_text: the estimated twinning stress, rtwinning, assuming c = 60 mJ m2, b = 0.25 nm and a Taylor factor of 2.8-3.2 [4], yields σ_ twinning ∼ γ / b = 670 - 750 MPa. This results in the development of twin bundles containing several deformation twins (Fig. 3(c)). The impingement of twin tips on coarse intergranular M_3 C-type carbides produces local shear stress concentrations at carbide interfaces, enhancing an intergranular fracture mode (Fig. 3(d)).

claim: The long annealed 2Al steel fractures at a macroscopic true stress of 1150 MPa, corresponding to the onset of the second hardening stage of the short annealed alloy, indicating that deformation twinning does not cause further hardening but activates damage via twin–M3C-type carbide interactions, resulting in a limited uniform elongation of 35%.
evidence_text: As Figure 2 shows, the fracture of the long annealed sample occurs at a macroscopic true stress of 1150 MPa, which corresponds to the onset of the second hardening regime of the short annealed alloy. As this hardening stage is associated with deformation twinning, this finding indicates that deformation twinning in the long annealed 2Al steel does not result in a further hardening regime but in the activation of a damage mechanism triggered by twin–intergranular M3C-type carbide interactions. As a consequence, the long annealed sample exhibits limited elongation (UE = 35%).

claim: The strain hardening of the long annealed 2Al alloy is described by a composite model: the grain interior (Area 1) consisting of dislocation substructures in nearly solid solution state controls the flow stress via Eq. (1), while grain boundary M3C-type carbides (Area 2) contribute to material damage, and Area 1 dominates the bulk strain hardening.
evidence_text: We can therefore analyze the strain hardening behavior of the long annealed 2Al alloy in terms of a composite-type microstructural model: the deformation structure of the grain interior (area 1), namely, dislocation substructures, contributes to the flow stress according to Eq. (1). This grain area is almost in the solid solution state (c phase). Segregation of solute elements, mainly carbon, to grain boundaries results in the precipitation of coarse M3C-type carbides, referred to as area 2. ... Area 2 mainly contributes to material damage. Under the annealing conditions studied here, area 1 is larger than area 2 and, hence, it controls the strain hardening behavior of the bulk material.

claim: Figure 2 shows that the short annealed 2Al alloy strain hardening rate decreases from 5.0 GPa at 400 MPa true stress to ~1.5 GPa at 1800 MPa, while the long annealed 2Al alloy decreases from 5.0 GPa at 400 MPa to ~1.7 GPa at 1200 MPa, with fracture at 1150 MPa.

In the long annealed 2Al alloy, the grain interior (area 1) containing dislocation substructures contributes to flow stress according to Eq. (1) and is almost in solid solution, while grain boundary M3C-type carbides (area 2) contribute to material damage; area 1 dominates the strain hardening behavior due to its larger volume.
evidence_text: the deformation structure of the grain interior (area 1), namely, dislocation substructures, contributes to the flow stress according to Eq. (1). This grain area is almost in the solid solution state (c phase). Segregation of solute elements, mainly carbon, to grain boundaries results in the precipitation of coarse M3C-type carbides, referred to as area 2. Accordingly, in this area, the following precipitation occurs: c phase to γ₀ phase + intergranular M₃C-type carbides. Area 2 mainly contributes to material damage.

claim: The short annealed 8Al steel tensile deformed to 0.3 true strain develops planar dislocation configurations including Taylor lattice structures and highly dense dislocation walls (HDDWs) that lie on the primary slip system and are spaced between 100 and 200 nm.
evidence_text: The short annealed 8Al steel develops planar dislocation configurations formed by Taylor lattice structures and highly dense dislocation walls (HDDWs) (Fig. 5(a)). This ECCI image shows the dislocation patterning of a short annealed 8Al steel sample tensile deformed to 0.3 true strain. Taylor lattice structures are formed by dense dislocation networks of planar dislocation arrays. These dislocation structures are characteristic of metals with pronounced planar slip. Figure 5(a) further reveals HDDWs consisting of dense dislocation sheets lying on the primary slip system and spaced between 100 and 200 nm.

claim: In the short annealed 8Al steel, a heterogeneous distribution of intragranular nanosized κ-carbides with sizes between 2 and 5 nm is observed, which have an L12-type structure and a cube/cube orientation relationship with the austenite matrix.
evidence_text: Figure 5(b) shows a dark-field TEM image of the precipitation state of the short annealed 8Al steel. In this annealing state, the precipitation distribution is heterogeneous, consisting of intragranular nanosized precipitates with sizes between 2 and 5 nm. Analysis of selected area diffraction patterns indicates that these precipitates are j-carbides containing an L0 1 -type structure, with a cube/cube orientation relationship with the austenite matrix.

claim: No dislocation–particle pinning events are observed in the short annealed 8Al steel; the nanosized κ-carbides are crystallographically sheared as weak obstacles, resulting in a moderate yield strength increase (YS_8Al = 540 MPa) compared to the 2Al steel (YS_2Al = 420 MPa) and promoting planar dislocation substructures.
evidence_text: Detailed TEM observations of the dislocation configurations do not reveal dislocation–particle pinning events. This finding indicates that, for materials that are in the annealed state studied here, 450°C/1 h, j-carbides can be considered as weak obstacles and, due to their tiny size, are crystallographically sheared. This effect results in a moderate increment of the yield strength compared to that of the 2Al steel in solid solution: YS_2Al steel = 420 MPa; YS_8Al steel = 540 MPa

claim: The long annealed 8Al steel contains a uniform distribution of intragranular cuboidal κ-carbides with average size 10–20 nm, maintaining a cube/cube orientation relationship and forming narrow matrix channels of 2–3 nm, which leads to a yield strength of 990 MPa compared to 420 MPa for the 2Al steel.
evidence_text: The long annealed 8Al steel contains a uniform distribution of intragranular cuboidal j-carbides with an average size of 10–20 nm (Fig. 5(d)). The analysis of the corresponding diffraction pattern confirms that these precipitates maintain a cube/cube orientation relationship with the austenite matrix. The high volume fraction of the precipitates results in the formation of narrow matrix channels, with a width of about 2–3 nm. This precipitation state yields a remarkable increase in the yield strength compared to that of the 2Al steel in solid solution: YS_2Al steel = 420 MPa; YS_8Al steel = 990 MPa.
